(**NBC**) is an [American](/wiki/American_television "American television") [commercial broadcast](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") [television](/wiki/Television_broadcaster "Television broadcaster") and [radio network](/wiki/Radio_network "Radio network"), serving as the flagship property of [NBC Entertainment](/wiki/NBCUniversal_Media_Group#NBC_Entertainment "NBCUniversal Media Group"), a division of [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal"), which is a subsidiary of [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"). It is one of NBCUniversal's two flagship subsidiaries, alongside [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Filmed_Entertainment_Group "Universal Filmed Entertainment Group"). It is the first and oldest major broadcast network in the United States.

in reference to its [stylized peacock logo](/wiki/NBC_logo "NBC logo"), which was introduced in 1956 to promote the company's innovations in early [color broadcasting](/wiki/Color_television "Color television").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The **National Broadcasting Company** (**NBC**) is an [American](/wiki/American_television "American television") [commercial broadcast](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") [television](/wiki/Television_broadcaster "Television broadcaster") and [radio network](/wiki/Radio_network "Radio network"), serving as the flagship property of [NBC Entertainment](/wiki/NBCUniversal_Media_Group#NBC_Entertainment "NBCUniversal Media Group"), a division of [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal"), which is a subsidiary of [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"). It is one of NBCUniversal's two flagship subsidiaries, alongside [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Filmed_Entertainment_Group "Universal Filmed Entertainment Group"). It is the first and oldest major broadcast network in the United States.

NBC's headquarters are located in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") at [Rockefeller Center](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center "Rockefeller Center")'s [Comcast Building](/wiki/30_Rockefeller_Plaza "30 Rockefeller Plaza"), the network's longtime home. The network's predecessor parent companies were integral to the center's construction. NBC also notably has offices at the [NBC Tower](/wiki/NBC_Tower "NBC Tower") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), Illinois, and at [10 Universal City Plaza](/wiki/10_Universal_City_Plaza "10 Universal City Plaza") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California.

Founded in 1926 by the [Radio Corporation of America](/wiki/RCA_Corporation "RCA Corporation") through its newly-created "National Broadcasting Company, Inc." division, then shortly thereafter co-owned by [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") (GE), [Westinghouse](/wiki/Westinghouse_Electric_Corporation "Westinghouse Electric Corporation"), and [AT&T Corporation](/wiki/AT%26T_Corporation "AT&T Corporation"), NBC is the oldest of the traditional "[Big Three](/wiki/Big_Three_(American_television) "Big Three (American television)")" American television networks in the 21st century (along with [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") and [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")) and is sometimes often referred to as the **Peacock Network** in reference to its [stylized peacock logo](/wiki/NBC_logo "NBC logo"), which was introduced in 1956 to promote the company's innovations in early [color broadcasting](/wiki/Color_television "Color television").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

NBC has twelve [owned-and-operated stations](/wiki/NBC_Owned_Television_Stations "NBC Owned Television Stations") and has [affiliates](/wiki/List_of_NBC_television_affiliates_(by_U.S._state) "List of NBC television affiliates (by U.S. state)") in almost every [TV market](/wiki/Media_market "Media market") in the United States. Some of the stations are also available in Mexico, the Caribbean, and Canada, via pay-television providers or in [border areas](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border") over the air.[[2]](#cite_note-NBCuni.com-2)

NBC was formed in 1926 by the [Radio Corporation of America](/wiki/Radio_Corporation_of_America "Radio Corporation of America") (RCA) as the first and oldest major broadcast network in the United States. NBC was then owned by [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") (GE), [Westinghouse](/wiki/Westinghouse_Electric_Corporation "Westinghouse Electric Corporation"), [AT&T Corporation](/wiki/AT%26T_Corporation "AT&T Corporation") and [United Fruit Company](/wiki/United_Fruit_Company "United Fruit Company"). In 1932, the [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") forced GE to sell RCA and NBC due to [antitrust](/wiki/Antitrust "Antitrust") violations. In late 1986, GE regained control of RCA through its $6.4 billion purchase of the company. Although it retained NBC, GE immediately closed or sold off most of RCA's other divisions and assets.

In 2004, French media company [Vivendi Universal](/wiki/Vivendi_Universal "Vivendi Universal") merged its entertainment assets ([Vivendi Universal Entertainment](/wiki/Vivendi_Universal_Entertainment "Vivendi Universal Entertainment")) with GE, forming [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal"). [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast") [purchased](/wiki/Acquisition_of_NBC_Universal_by_Comcast "Acquisition of NBC Universal by Comcast") a controlling interest in NBCUniversal in 2011 and acquired GE's remaining stake in 2013.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/GE_Building_Oct_2005.jpg/250px-GE_Building_Oct_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:GE_Building_Oct_2005.jpg)

[30 Rockefeller Plaza](/wiki/30_Rockefeller_Plaza "30 Rockefeller Plaza"), the headquarters of NBC at [Rockefeller Center](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center "Rockefeller Center") in New York City

NBC is the home broadcaster of some of the [longest continuously running American television series](/wiki/List_of_longest-running_American_television_series "List of longest-running American television series"), including the news program *[Meet the Press](/wiki/Meet_the_Press "Meet the Press")* (debuted 1947); *[Today](/wiki/Today_(American_TV_program) "Today (American TV program)")* (debuted 1952); *[The Tonight Show](/wiki/The_Tonight_Show "The Tonight Show")* (debuted nationally 1954); and *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* (debuted 1975). The drama series *[Law & Order: Special Victims Unit](/wiki/Law_%26_Order:_Special_Victims_Unit "Law & Order: Special Victims Unit")*, which debuted in 1999, began its 26th season in October 2024 and is currently [the longest-running live-action series](/wiki/List_of_longest-running_scripted_American_primetime_television_series "List of longest-running scripted American primetime television series") in American prime-time television history.

## Programming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Programming")]

Main articles: [List of programs broadcast by NBC](/wiki/List_of_programs_broadcast_by_NBC "List of programs broadcast by NBC"), [List of programs previously broadcast by NBC](/wiki/List_of_programs_previously_broadcast_by_NBC "List of programs previously broadcast by NBC"), [NBC News](/wiki/NBC_News "NBC News"), and [NBC Sports](/wiki/NBC_Sports "NBC Sports")

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit), NBC provides 87 hours of regularly scheduled network programming each week. The network provides 22 hours of prime-time programming to affiliated stations Monday through Saturdays from 8:00 p.m. to 11:00 p.m. Eastern and Pacific Time (7:00 p.m.–10:00 p.m. in all other U.S. time zones) and Sundays from 7:00 p.m. to 11:00 p.m. Eastern and Pacific Time (6:00 p.m.–10:00 p.m. in all other time zones).

Daytime [NBC News](/wiki/NBC_News "NBC News") programming includes the morning news/interview program *[Today](/wiki/Today_(American_TV_program) "Today (American TV program)")* from 7:00 a.m. to 11:00 a.m. weekdays, 7:00 a.m.–8:30 a.m. / 8:00 a.m. - 9:30 a.m. on Saturdays and 7:00 a.m.–8:00 a.m. / 8:00 a.m. -9:00 a.m. on Sundays, it also airs *[NBC News Daily](/wiki/NBC_News_Daily "NBC News Daily")* at 12:00 p.m.–1:00 p.m. on weekdays, it includes nightly editions of *[NBC Nightly News](/wiki/NBC_Nightly_News "NBC Nightly News")*, the [Sunday political talk show](/wiki/Sunday_morning_talk_show "Sunday morning talk show") *[Meet the Press](/wiki/Meet_the_Press "Meet the Press")*, weekday early-morning news program *[Early Today](/wiki/Early_Today "Early Today")* and primetime newsmagazine *[Dateline NBC](/wiki/Dateline_NBC "Dateline NBC") on Friday nights*. Late nights feature the weeknight talk shows *[The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon](/wiki/The_Tonight_Show_Starring_Jimmy_Fallon "The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon")*, *[Late Night with Seth Meyers](/wiki/Late_Night_with_Seth_Meyers "Late Night with Seth Meyers")*, and [overnight](/wiki/Graveyard_slot "Graveyard slot") replays of *Nightly News* and NBC News Now's primetime newscast *Top Story with Tom Llamas.* NBC affiliates carrying it in syndication also have the option to substitute a same-day encore of *[The Kelly Clarkson Show](/wiki/The_Kelly_Clarkson_Show "The Kelly Clarkson Show")* on weekdays. On Saturdays, the [LXTV](/wiki/LXTV "LXTV")-produced *[1st Look](/wiki/1st_Look "1st Look")* and *Open House NYC* air after *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* (replays of the previous week's *1st Look* also air on Friday late nights on most stations), with a *Meet the Press* encore a part of its Sunday overnight schedule.

The network's weekend morning children's programming time slot is programmed by [Litton Entertainment](/wiki/Litton_Entertainment "Litton Entertainment") under a time-lease agreement. The three-hour block of programming designed mainly for 14-16-year-old teenage viewers is under the umbrella branding of *[The More You Know](/wiki/The_More_You_Know_(TV_programming_block) "The More You Know (TV programming block)")*, based on the network's long-time strand of internally-produced [public service announcements](/wiki/Public_service_announcement "Public service announcement") of [the same name](/wiki/The_More_You_Know "The More You Know"). It premiered on October 8, 2016, giving Litton control of all but Fox's Weekend morning [E/I](/wiki/E/I "E/I") programming among the five major broadcast networks.

Live sports programming is also provided on weekends at any time between 7:00 a.m. and 1:30 a.m. Eastern Time, but most commonly between 12 p.m. and 6 p.m. Eastern (and from 7 and 11 p.m. on some Saturdays and all Sundays since Fall 2025 as part of its Sunday Year round sports programming block). Due to the unpredictable length of sporting events, NBC will occasionally pre-empt scheduled programs (more common with the weekend editions of *NBC Nightly News*, and local and syndicated programs carried by its owned-and-operated stations and affiliates). NBC has also held the American broadcasting rights to the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") since the 1988 games and the rights to the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") since the 2002 games. Coverage of the [Olympics on NBC](/wiki/Olympics_on_NBC "Olympics on NBC") has included pre-empting regularly scheduled programs during daytime, prime time, and late night. In July 2022, NBC announced that the Olympic Channel will be shut down on September 30. NBC stated they will be announcing the plans for Olympic content in the fall of 2022.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### NBC News

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: NBC News")]

Main article: [NBC News](/wiki/NBC_News "NBC News")

News coverage has long been an important part of NBC's operations and public image, dating to the network's radio days. Notable NBC News productions past and present include *Today*, *NBC Nightly News* (and its immediate predecessor, *[The Huntley–Brinkley Report](/wiki/The_Huntley%E2%80%93Brinkley_Report "The Huntley–Brinkley Report")*), *Meet the Press* (which has the distinction of the longest continuously running program in the history of American television), *Dateline NBC*, *Early Today*, *[NBC News at Sunrise](/wiki/NBC_News_at_Sunrise "NBC News at Sunrise")*, *[NBC Nightside](/wiki/NBC_Nightside "NBC Nightside")* and *[Rock Center with Brian Williams](/wiki/Rock_Center_with_Brian_Williams "Rock Center with Brian Williams")*.

In 1989, the news division began its expansion to cable with the launch of the business news channel [CNBC](/wiki/CNBC "CNBC"). The company eventually formed other [cable news](/wiki/United_States_cable_news "United States cable news") services including [MSNBC](/wiki/MSNBC "MSNBC") (created in 1996 originally as a joint venture with [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), which now features a mix of general news and political discussion programs with a liberal stance),[[5]](#cite_note-Auto8E-8-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Auto8E-9-6) and the 2008 acquisition of [The Weather Channel](/wiki/The_Weather_Channel "The Weather Channel") in conjunction with [Blackstone Group](/wiki/Blackstone_Group "Blackstone Group") and [Bain Capital](/wiki/Bain_Capital "Bain Capital"). In addition, [NBCSN](/wiki/NBCSN "NBCSN") (operated as part of the NBC Sports Group, which became an NBC property through Comcast's acquisition of [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal")) carries sports news content alongside sports event telecasts. Key anchors from NBC News are also used during NBC Sports coverage of the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games").

### Former Daytime programming block

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Former Daytime programming block")]

Main article: [NBC Daytime](/wiki/NBC_Daytime "NBC Daytime")

While NBC has aired a variety of soap operas on its daytime schedule over its history, *[Days of Our Lives](/wiki/Days_of_Our_Lives "Days of Our Lives")* (1965–2022) was the last soap opera on the network when it was taken off the air in 2022 (and moved to the [Peacock](/wiki/Peacock_(streaming_service) "Peacock (streaming service)") streaming service). Currently the network only offers *[NBC News Daily](/wiki/NBC_News_Daily "NBC News Daily")* on its afternoon schedule, with affiliates using the rest of the afternoon for syndicated or local programming.

Long-running daytime dramas seen on NBC in the past include *[The Doctors](/wiki/The_Doctors_(1963_TV_series) "The Doctors (1963 TV series)")* (1963–1982), *[Another World](/wiki/Another_World_(TV_series) "Another World (TV series)")* (1964–1999), *[Santa Barbara](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_(TV_series) "Santa Barbara (TV series)")* (1984–1993), and *[Passions](/wiki/Passions "Passions")* (1999–2007). NBC also aired the final 41⁄2 years of *[Search for Tomorrow](/wiki/Search_for_Tomorrow "Search for Tomorrow")* (1982–1986) after that series was initially cancelled by CBS, although many NBC affiliates did not clear the show during its tenure on the network. NBC has also aired numerous short-lived soap operas, including *[Generations](/wiki/Generations_(American_TV_series) "Generations (American TV series)")* (1989–1991), *[Sunset Beach](/wiki/Sunset_Beach_(TV_series) "Sunset Beach (TV series)")* (1997–1999), and the two *Another World* spin-offs, *[Somerset](/wiki/Somerset_(TV_series) "Somerset (TV series)")* (1970–1976) and *[Texas](/wiki/Texas_(TV_series) "Texas (TV series)")* (1980–1982).

Notable daytime game shows that once aired on NBC include *[The Price Is Right](/wiki/The_Price_Is_Right_(1956_American_game_show) "The Price Is Right (1956 American game show)")* (1956–1963), *[Concentration](/wiki/Concentration_(game_show) "Concentration (game show)")* (1958–1973; and 1987–1991 as *Classic Concentration*), *[The Match Game](/wiki/Match_Game "Match Game")* (1962–1969), *[Let's Make a Deal](/wiki/Let%27s_Make_a_Deal "Let's Make a Deal")* (1963–1968 and 1990–1991, as well as a short-lived prime-time revival in 2003), *[Jeopardy!](/wiki/Jeopardy! "Jeopardy!")* (1964–1975 and 1978–1979), *[The Hollywood Squares](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Squares "The Hollywood Squares")* (1966–1980), *[Wheel of Fortune](/wiki/Wheel_of_Fortune_(American_game_show) "Wheel of Fortune (American game show)")* (1975–1989 and 1991), *[Password Plus/Super Password](/wiki/Password_Plus_and_Super_Password "Password Plus and Super Password")* (1979–1982 and 1984–1989), *[Sale of the Century](/wiki/Sale_of_the_Century "Sale of the Century")* (1969–1973 and 1983–1989) and *[Scrabble](/wiki/Scrabble_(game_show) "Scrabble (game show)")* (1984–1990 and 1993). The last game show ever to air as part of NBC's daytime schedule was the short-lived *[Caesars Challenge](/wiki/Caesars_Challenge "Caesars Challenge")*, which ended in January 1994.

Notable past daytime talk shows that have aired on NBC have included *[Home](/wiki/Home_(1954_TV_series) "Home (1954 TV series)")* (1954–1957), *[The Ernie Kovacs Show](/wiki/The_Ernie_Kovacs_Show "The Ernie Kovacs Show")* (1955–1956), *[The Merv Griffin Show](/wiki/The_Merv_Griffin_Show "The Merv Griffin Show")* (1962–1963), *[Leeza](/wiki/Leeza "Leeza")* (1994–1999) and *[Later Today](/wiki/Later_Today "Later Today")* (1999–2000).

### Children's programming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Children's programming")]

Main articles: [Children's programming on NBC](/wiki/Children%27s_programming_on_NBC "Children's programming on NBC"), [TNBC](/wiki/TNBC "TNBC"), [Discovery Kids on NBC](/wiki/Discovery_Kids_on_NBC "Discovery Kids on NBC"), [Qubo](/wiki/Qubo "Qubo"), [NBC Kids](/wiki/NBC_Kids "NBC Kids"), and [The More You Know (TV programming block)](/wiki/The_More_You_Know_(TV_programming_block) "The More You Know (TV programming block)")

Children's programming has played a part in NBC's programming since its initial roots in television. NBC's first major children's series, *[Howdy Doody](/wiki/Howdy_Doody "Howdy Doody")*, debuted in 1947 and was one of the era's first breakthrough television shows. From the mid-1960s until 1992, the bulk of NBC's children's programming was composed of mainly animated programming including classic *[Looney Tunes](/wiki/Looney_Tunes "Looney Tunes")* and *[Woody Woodpecker](/wiki/Woody_Woodpecker "Woody Woodpecker")* shorts; reruns of prime time animated sitcoms such as *[The Flintstones](/wiki/The_Flintstones "The Flintstones")* and *[The Jetsons](/wiki/The_Jetsons "The Jetsons")*; foreign acquisitions like *[Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy")* and *[Kimba the White Lion](/wiki/Kimba_the_White_Lion "Kimba the White Lion")*; animated adaptions of *[Punky Brewster](/wiki/Punky_Brewster "Punky Brewster")*, *[ALF](/wiki/ALF_(TV_series) "ALF (TV series)")* and *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")* as well as animated vehicles for [Gary Coleman](/wiki/Gary_Coleman "Gary Coleman") and [Mr. T](/wiki/Mr._T "Mr. T"); live-action programs like *[The Banana Splits](/wiki/The_Banana_Splits "The Banana Splits")*, *[The Bugaloos](/wiki/The_Bugaloos "The Bugaloos")* and *[H.R. Pufnstuf](/wiki/H.R._Pufnstuf "H.R. Pufnstuf")*; and the original broadcasts of *[Gumby](/wiki/Gumby "Gumby")*, *[The Rocky and Bullwinkle Show](/wiki/The_Rocky_and_Bullwinkle_Show "The Rocky and Bullwinkle Show")*, *[Underdog](/wiki/Underdog_(TV_series) "Underdog (TV series)")*, *[The Smurfs](/wiki/The_Smurfs_(1981_TV_series) "The Smurfs (1981 TV series)")*, *[Alvin and the Chipmunks](/wiki/Alvin_and_the_Chipmunks_(1983_TV_series) "Alvin and the Chipmunks (1983 TV series)")* and *[Disney's Adventures of the Gummi Bears](/wiki/Disney%27s_Adventures_of_the_Gummi_Bears "Disney's Adventures of the Gummi Bears")*. From 1984 to 1989, the network aired a series of [public service announcements](/wiki/Public_service_announcement "Public service announcement") called *[One to Grow On](/wiki/One_to_Grow_On "One to Grow On")*, which aired after the end credits of every program or every other children's program.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1989, NBC premiered *[Saved by the Bell](/wiki/Saved_by_the_Bell "Saved by the Bell")*, a live-action [teen sitcom](/wiki/Teen_sitcom "Teen sitcom") which originated on [The Disney Channel](/wiki/Disney_Channel "Disney Channel") the previous year as *[Good Morning, Miss Bliss](/wiki/Good_Morning,_Miss_Bliss "Good Morning, Miss Bliss")* (which served as a starring vehicle for [Hayley Mills](/wiki/Hayley_Mills "Hayley Mills"); [four](/wiki/Mark-Paul_Gosselaar "Mark-Paul Gosselaar") [cast members](/wiki/Dennis_Haskins "Dennis Haskins") [from](/wiki/Lark_Voorhies "Lark Voorhies") [that show](/wiki/Dustin_Diamond "Dustin Diamond") were cast in the NBC series as the characters they originally played on *Miss Bliss*). *Saved by the Bell*, despite being given bad reviews from television critics, would become one of the most popular teen series in television history as well as the top-rated series on Saturday mornings, dethroning ABC's *[The Bugs Bunny and Tweety Show](/wiki/The_Bugs_Bunny_Show "The Bugs Bunny Show")* in its first season.

The success of *Saved by the Bell* led NBC to remove animated series from its Saturday morning lineup in August 1992 in favor of additional live-action series as part of a new block called [TNBC](/wiki/TNBC "TNBC"), along with the debut of a Saturday edition of *[Today](/wiki/Weekend_Today "Weekend Today")*. Most of the series featured on the TNBC lineup were executive produced by [Peter Engel](/wiki/Peter_Engel_(TV_producer) "Peter Engel (TV producer)") (such as *[City Guys](/wiki/City_Guys "City Guys")*, *[Hang Time](/wiki/Hang_Time_(TV_series) "Hang Time (TV series)")*, *[California Dreams](/wiki/California_Dreams "California Dreams")*, *One World* and the *Saved by the Bell* sequel, *[Saved by the Bell: The New Class](/wiki/Saved_by_the_Bell:_The_New_Class "Saved by the Bell: The New Class")*), with the lineup being designed from the start to meet the earliest form of the FCC's [educational programming guidelines](/wiki/E/I "E/I") under the [Children's Television Act](/wiki/Children%27s_Television_Act "Children's Television Act").[[8]](#cite_note-DiscoveryKids-8) *NBA Inside Stuff*, an analysis and interview program aimed at teens that was hosted for most of its run by [Ahmad Rashad](/wiki/Ahmad_Rashad "Ahmad Rashad"), was also a part of the TNBC lineup during the NBA season until 2002 (when the program moved to ABC as a result of that network taking the NBA rights from NBC).

In 2002, NBC entered into an agreement with [Discovery Communications](/wiki/Discovery,_Inc. "Discovery, Inc.") to carry educational children's programs from the [Discovery Kids](/wiki/Discovery_Kids "Discovery Kids") cable channel.[[8]](#cite_note-DiscoveryKids-8) Debuting that September, the [Discovery Kids on NBC](/wiki/Discovery_Kids_on_NBC "Discovery Kids on NBC") block originally consisted exclusively of live-action series, including reality series *Trading Spaces: Boys vs. Girls* (a kid-themed version of the [TLC](/wiki/TLC_(TV_network) "TLC (TV network)") series *[Trading Spaces](/wiki/Trading_Spaces "Trading Spaces")*); the Emmy-nominated reality game show *[Endurance](/wiki/Endurance_(TV_series) "Endurance (TV series)")*, hosted and produced by [J. D. Roth](/wiki/J._D._Roth "J. D. Roth") (whose production company, 3-Ball Productions, would also produce reality series *[The Biggest Loser](/wiki/The_Biggest_Loser_(American_TV_series) "The Biggest Loser (American TV series)")* for NBC beginning in 2003); and scripted series such as *[Strange Days at Blake Holsey High](/wiki/Strange_Days_at_Blake_Holsey_High "Strange Days at Blake Holsey High")* and *[Scout's Safari](/wiki/Scout%27s_Safari "Scout's Safari")*. The block later expanded to include some animated series such as *[Kenny the Shark](/wiki/Kenny_the_Shark "Kenny the Shark")*, *[Tutenstein](/wiki/Tutenstein "Tutenstein")* and *[Time Warp Trio](/wiki/Time_Warp_Trio "Time Warp Trio")*.

In May 2006, NBC announced plans to launch a new Saturday morning children's block under the [Qubo](/wiki/Qubo "Qubo") brand in September 2006.[[9]](#cite_note-9) An endeavor originally operated as a joint venture between [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal"), [Ion Media Networks](/wiki/Ion_Media_Networks "Ion Media Networks"), [Scholastic Press](/wiki/Scholastic_Press "Scholastic Press"), [Classic Media](/wiki/Classic_Media "Classic Media") and [Corus Entertainment](/wiki/Corus_Entertainment "Corus Entertainment")'s [Nelvana](/wiki/Nelvana "Nelvana") unit (Ion acquired the other partners' shares in 2013), the Qubo venture also encompassed weekly blocks on Telemundo and [Ion Television](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television"), a 24-hour digital multicast network on Ion's owned-and-operated and affiliated stations, as well as [video on demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand") services and a branded website. Qubo launched on NBC on September 9, 2006, with six programs (*[VeggieTales](/wiki/VeggieTales "VeggieTales")*, *[Dragon](/wiki/Dragon_(TV_series) "Dragon (TV series)")*, *VeggieTales Presents: [3-2-1 Penguins!](/wiki/3-2-1_Penguins! "3-2-1 Penguins!")*, *[Babar](/wiki/Babar_(TV_series) "Babar (TV series)")*, *[Jane and the Dragon](/wiki/Jane_and_the_Dragon_(TV_series) "Jane and the Dragon (TV series)")* and *[Jacob Two-Two](/wiki/Jacob_Two-Two_(TV_series) "Jacob Two-Two (TV series)")*).

On March 28, 2012, it was announced that NBC would launch a new Saturday morning preschool block programmed by Sprout (originally jointly owned by NBCUniversal, [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS"), [Sesame Workshop](/wiki/Sesame_Workshop "Sesame Workshop") and [Apax Partners](/wiki/Apax_Partners "Apax Partners"), with the former acquiring the other's interests later that year). The block, [NBC Kids](/wiki/NBC_Kids "NBC Kids"), premiered on July 7, 2012, replacing the "Qubo on NBC" block.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

On February 24, 2016, it was announced that NBC would launch a new Saturday morning block programmed by [Litton Entertainment](/wiki/Hearst_Media_Production_Group "Hearst Media Production Group") under the Children's Television Act. It's called [The More You Know](/wiki/The_More_You_Know_(TV_programming_block) "The More You Know (TV programming block)"), inspired by the name of [brand extension](/wiki/Brand_extension "Brand extension") of *[The More You Know](/wiki/The_More_You_Know "The More You Know")*—a series of [public service](/wiki/Public_service_announcement "Public service announcement") campaigns first launched by NBC in 1989.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-band-more_you_know-15) The block premiered on October 8, 2016, replacing NBC Kids block (originally October 1, 2016, but postponed due to the [NBC network coverage](/wiki/Golf_on_NBC "Golf on NBC") of the [2016 Ryder Cup](/wiki/2016_Ryder_Cup "2016 Ryder Cup")).

### Specials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Specials")]

NBC holds the broadcast rights to several annual specials and award show telecasts, including the [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") and the [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award") (which are rotated across all four major networks each year). Since 1953, NBC has served as the official American broadcaster of the [Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade](/wiki/Macy%27s_Thanksgiving_Day_Parade "Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade"). CBS also carries unauthorized coverage of the Macy's parade as part of *The Thanksgiving Day Parade on CBS*; however, as NBC holds rights to the parade, it has exclusivity over the broadcast of Broadway and music performances appearing in the parade (CBS airs live performances separate from those seen in the parade as a result), and Macy's chose to reroute the parade in 2012 out of the view of CBS' cameras, although it continues to cover the parade. NBC began airing a same-day rebroadcast of the parade telecast in 2009 (replacing its annual Thanksgiving afternoon airing of *[Miracle on 34th Street](/wiki/Miracle_on_34th_Street "Miracle on 34th Street")*). In 2007, NBC acquired the rights to the [National Dog Show](/wiki/National_Dog_Show "National Dog Show"), which airs following the Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade each year.

The network also broadcasts several live-action and animated specials during the [Christmas holiday season](/wiki/Christmas_holiday_season "Christmas holiday season"), including the 2014 debuts *[How Murray Saved Christmas](/wiki/How_Murray_Saved_Christmas "How Murray Saved Christmas")* (an animated musical adaptation of the children's book of the same name) and *[Elf: Buddy's Musical Christmas](/wiki/Elf:_Buddy%27s_Musical_Christmas "Elf: Buddy's Musical Christmas")* (a stop-motion animated special based on the 2003 live-action film *[Elf](/wiki/Elf_(film) "Elf (film)")*).

Since 2013, the network has aired live musical adaptations with major stars in lead roles. Originally dismissed as a gimmick, they have proven to be rating successes, as well as a nostalgic tribute to the early days of television. Past adaptations include:

* *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music_Live! "The Sound of Music Live!")* in 2013 (starring [Carrie Underwood](/wiki/Carrie_Underwood "Carrie Underwood") as Maria Von Trapp)
* *[Peter Pan](/wiki/Peter_Pan_Live! "Peter Pan Live!")* in 2014 (starring [Allison Williams](/wiki/Allison_Williams_(actress) "Allison Williams (actress)") in the titular role and [Christopher Walken](/wiki/Christopher_Walken "Christopher Walken") as Captain Hook)
* *[The Wiz](/wiki/The_Wiz_Live! "The Wiz Live!")* in 2015 (starring [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah") as the Wiz, [Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige") as the Wicked Witch and [Uzo Aduba](/wiki/Uzo_Aduba "Uzo Aduba") as the Good Witch)
* *[Hairspray](/wiki/Hairspray_Live! "Hairspray Live!")* in 2016 (starring [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande") as Penny Pingleton, [Jennifer Hudson](/wiki/Jennifer_Hudson "Jennifer Hudson") as Motormouth Maybelle, [Kristin Chenoweth](/wiki/Kristin_Chenoweth "Kristin Chenoweth") as Velma von Tussle and [Harvey Fierstein](/wiki/Harvey_Fierstein "Harvey Fierstein") as Edna Turnblad, reprising his role in [the original Broadway production](/wiki/Hairspray_(musical) "Hairspray (musical)"))
* *[Jesus Christ Superstar](/wiki/Jesus_Christ_Superstar_Live_in_Concert "Jesus Christ Superstar Live in Concert")* in 2018 (starring [John Legend](/wiki/John_Legend "John Legend") as Jesus Christ, [Sara Bareilles](/wiki/Sara_Bareilles "Sara Bareilles") as Mary Magdalene and [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper") as King Herod)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* *[Annie Live!](/wiki/Annie_Live! "Annie Live!")* in 2021 (starring [Taraji P. Henson](/wiki/Taraji_P._Henson "Taraji P. Henson") as Miss Hannigan, [Harry Connick Jr.](/wiki/Harry_Connick_Jr. "Harry Connick Jr.") as Daddy Warbucks, [Nicole Scherzinger](/wiki/Nicole_Scherzinger "Nicole Scherzinger") as Grace Farrell and [Tituss Burgess](/wiki/Tituss_Burgess "Tituss Burgess") as Rooster Hannigan)

From 2003 to 2014, NBC also held rights to two of the three pageants organized by the [Miss Universe Organization](/wiki/Miss_Universe_Organization "Miss Universe Organization"): the [Miss Universe](/wiki/Miss_Universe "Miss Universe") and [Miss USA](/wiki/Miss_USA "Miss USA") pageants (NBC also held rights to the [Miss Teen USA](/wiki/Miss_Teen_USA "Miss Teen USA") pageant from 2003, when NBC also assumed rights to the Miss USA and Miss Universe pageants as part of a deal brokered by Miss Universe Organization owner [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") that gave the network half-ownership of the pageants,[[17]](#cite_note-17) until 2007, when NBC declined to renew its contract to carry Miss Teen USA, effectively discontinuing televised broadcasts of that event until 2023). NBCUniversal relinquished the rights to Miss Universe and Miss USA on June 29, 2015, as part of its decision to cut business ties with Donald Trump and the Miss Universe Organization (which was half-owned by corporate parent NBCUniversal) in response to controversial remarks about Mexican immigrants made by Trump during the launch of his [2016 campaign for the Republican presidential nomination](/wiki/Donald_Trump_presidential_campaign,_2016 "Donald Trump presidential campaign, 2016").[[18]](#cite_note-deadline-nbcdone-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Programming library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Programming library")]

Through the years, NBC has produced many in-house programs, in addition to airing content from other producers such as [Revue Studios](/wiki/Universal_Television "Universal Television") and its successor [Universal Television](/wiki/Universal_Television "Universal Television"), along with [CBS Studios](/wiki/CBS_Studios "CBS Studios") and [Paramount Television Studios](/wiki/Paramount_Television_Studios "Paramount Television Studios"), both unit of [Paramount Skydance](/wiki/Paramount_Skydance "Paramount Skydance") which currently owns the rights to the pre-1973 NBC's in-house programming library. Notable in-house productions by NBC have included *[Bonanza](/wiki/Bonanza "Bonanza")*, *[Destination X](/wiki/Destination_X_(TV_series) "Destination X (TV series)")*, *[Little House on the Prairie](/wiki/Little_House_on_the_Prairie_(TV_series) "Little House on the Prairie (TV series)")*, *[The Voice](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_TV_series) "The Voice (American TV series)")*, *[Dateline](/wiki/Dateline "Dateline")*, *[Yes, Chef!](/wiki/Yes,_Chef! "Yes, Chef!")* *[Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas_(TV_series) "Las Vegas (TV series)")*, *[Crossing Jordan](/wiki/Crossing_Jordan "Crossing Jordan")*, *[Transplant](/wiki/Transplant_(TV_series) "Transplant (TV series)")*, the [*Law & Order* franchise](/wiki/Law_%26_Order_(franchise) "Law & Order (franchise)") (begun independently by Universal Television, and became in-house programming after the NBCUniversal deal), *[The Office](/wiki/The_Office_(American_TV_series) "The Office (American TV series)")*, *[Deal or No Deal Island](/wiki/Deal_or_No_Deal_Island "Deal or No Deal Island")* and the [*Chicago* franchise](/wiki/Chicago_(franchise) "Chicago (franchise)").

## Stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Stations")]

Main articles: [List of NBC television affiliates](/wiki/List_of_NBC_television_affiliates "List of NBC television affiliates") and [NBC Owned Television Stations](/wiki/NBC_Owned_Television_Stations "NBC Owned Television Stations")

NBC has twelve owned-and-operated stations and current and pending affiliation agreements with 222 additional television stations encompassing 50 states, the District of Columbia, six U.S. possessions and two non-U.S. territories ([Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba") and [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda")).[[20]](#cite_note-re-20)[[21]](#cite_note-StationIndex-21) The network has a national reach of 88.91% of all households in the United States (or 277,821,345 Americans with at least one television set). From January 24, 2022, when [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") affiliate [WBKB-TV](/wiki/WBKB-TV "WBKB-TV") in [Alpena, Michigan](/wiki/Alpena,_Michigan "Alpena, Michigan") affiliated its DT2 subchannel with NBC, to December 31, 2024, when [KXGN-TV](/wiki/KXGN-TV "KXGN-TV") in [Glendive, Montana](/wiki/Glendive,_Montana "Glendive, Montana") dropped NBC from its DT2 subchannel, NBC was the only major network with an in-market affiliate in every [designated market area](/wiki/Media_market "Media market") in the United States.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-grr-kxgnnonbc-23)

Currently, [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") are the only U.S. states where NBC does not have a locally licensed affiliate. New Jersey is served by New York City O&O WNBC-TV and Philadelphia O&O WCAU; New Jersey formerly had an in-state affiliate in [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey")-based [WMGM-TV](/wiki/WMGM-TV "WMGM-TV"), which was affiliated with the network from 1966 to 2014. Delaware is served by Salisbury affiliate [WRDE-LD](/wiki/WRDE-LD "WRDE-LD") and Philadelphia-based [WCAU](/wiki/WCAU "WCAU"). NBC maintains affiliations with low-power stations in a few smaller markets, such as [Binghamton, New York](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York") ([WBGH-CD](/wiki/WBGH-CD "WBGH-CD")), [Jackson, Tennessee](/wiki/Jackson,_Tennessee "Jackson, Tennessee") ([WNBJ-LD](/wiki/WNBJ-LD "WNBJ-LD")) and [Juneau, Alaska](/wiki/Juneau,_Alaska "Juneau, Alaska") ([KATH-LD](/wiki/KATH-LD "KATH-LD")), that do not have enough full-power stations to support a standalone affiliate. In some markets, these stations also maintain digital simulcasts on a subchannel of a co-owned/co-managed full-power television station.

Southern [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") receives NBC programming via network-owned [WBTS-CD](/wiki/WBTS-CD "WBTS-CD"), licensed to serve [Nashua](/wiki/Nashua,_New_Hampshire "Nashua, New Hampshire"); while nominally licensed as a low-power [class A](/wiki/Class_A_television_service "Class A television service") station, it transmits a full-power signal under a channel share with the [WGBH Educational Foundation](/wiki/WGBH_Educational_Foundation "WGBH Educational Foundation") and its secondary Boston station [WGBX-TV](/wiki/WGBX-TV "WGBX-TV") from [Needham, Massachusetts](/wiki/Needham,_Massachusetts "Needham, Massachusetts"), and serves as the NBC station for the entire Boston market. Until 2019, NBC operated a low-powered station in Boston, WBTS-LD (now [WYCN-LD](/wiki/WYCN-LD "WYCN-LD")), which aimed to serve as its station in that market while using a network of additional full-power stations to cover the market in full (including [Merrimack, New Hampshire](/wiki/Merrimack,_New_Hampshire "Merrimack, New Hampshire")-licensed [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") station [WNEU](/wiki/WNEU "WNEU"), which transmitted WBTS on a second subchannel); NBC purchased the Nashua station (formerly WYCN-CD) in early 2018 after the FCC [spectrum auction](/wiki/Spectrum_auction "Spectrum auction"), and in 2019 relocated WYCN-LD to Providence, Rhode Island to serve as a Telemundo station for that market.

[Tegna Media](/wiki/Tegna_Inc. "Tegna Inc.") is the largest operator of NBC stations in terms of overall market reach, owning or providing services to 20 NBC affiliates (including those in larger markets such as [Atlanta](/wiki/WXIA-TV "WXIA-TV"), [Denver](/wiki/KUSA_(TV) "KUSA (TV)"), [St. Louis](/wiki/KSDK "KSDK"), [Seattle](/wiki/KING-TV "KING-TV") and [Cleveland](/wiki/WKYC-TV "WKYC-TV")); [Gray Television](/wiki/Gray_Television "Gray Television") is the largest owner and operator of NBC stations by quantity and numerical total, owning 64 NBC-affiliated stations.

## Related services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Related services")]

### Video-on-demand services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Video-on-demand services")]

NBC provides [video on demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand") access for delayed viewing of the network's programming through various means, including via its website at NBC.com, a traditional VOD service called NBC on Demand available on most traditional cable and IPTV providers,[[24]](#cite_note-24) and through content deals with [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") and [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") (the latter of which carries only cataloged episodes of NBC programs, after losing the right to carry newer episodes of its programs during their current seasons in July 2011). From 2007 to 2025, [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") was a part-owner of Hulu (along with majority owner [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company"), owner of ABC), and has offered full-length episodes of most of NBC's programming through the [streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") service (which are available for viewing on Hulu's website and [mobile app](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app")) since Hulu launched in [private beta testing](/wiki/Beta_testing "Beta testing") on October 29, 2007.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The most recent episodes of the network's shows are usually made available on NBC.com and Hulu the day after their original broadcast. In addition, NBC.com and certain other partner websites (including Hulu) provide complete back catalogs of most of its current series as well as a limited selection of episodes of classic series from the NBCUniversal Television Distribution program library – including shows not broadcast by NBC during their original runs (including the complete or partial episode catalogs of shows like *30 Rock*, *The A-Team*, *[Charles in Charge](/wiki/Charles_in_Charge "Charles in Charge")*, *[Emergency!](/wiki/Emergency! "Emergency!")*, *Knight Rider* (both the original series and the [short-lived 2008 reboot](/wiki/Knight_Rider_(2008_TV_series) "Knight Rider (2008 TV series)")), *[Kojak](/wiki/Kojak "Kojak")*, *Miami Vice*, *[The Office](/wiki/The_Office_(American_TV_series) "The Office (American TV series)")*, *[Quantum Leap](/wiki/Quantum_Leap_(1989_TV_series) "Quantum Leap (1989 TV series)")* and *[Simon & Simon](/wiki/Simon_%26_Simon "Simon & Simon")*).[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

On February 18, 2015, NBC began providing live programming streams of local NBC stations in select markets, which are only available to [authenticated subscribers of participating pay television providers](/wiki/TV_Everywhere "TV Everywhere"). All eleven NBC-owned-and-operated stations owned by NBCUniversal Owned Television Stations' were the first stations to offer streams of their programming on NBC's website and mobile app, and new affiliation agreements have made a majority of the network's affiliates available through the network's website and app based on a viewer's location. The network's NFL game telecasts were [not permitted to be streamed](/wiki/Blackout_(broadcasting) "Blackout (broadcasting)") on the service for several years until a change to the league's mobile rights agreement in the 2018 season allowed games to be streamed through network websites and apps.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### NBC HD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: NBC HD")]

NBC's master feed is transmitted in [1080i](/wiki/1080i "1080i") [high definition](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television"), the native resolution format for NBCUniversal's television properties. However, 19 of its affiliates transmit the network's programming in [720p](/wiki/720p "720p") HD, while four others carry the network feed in [480i](/wiki/480i "480i") [standard definition](/wiki/Standard-definition_television "Standard-definition television")[[20]](#cite_note-re-20) either due to technical considerations for affiliates of other major networks that carry NBC programming on a digital subchannel or because a primary feed NBC affiliate has not yet upgraded their transmission equipment to allow content to be presented in HD.

NBC's master feed has not fully converted to [1080p](/wiki/1080p "1080p") or [2160p](/wiki/2160p "2160p") [ultra-high-definition television](/wiki/Ultra-high-definition_television "Ultra-high-definition television") (UHD). However, some NBC stations have already begun broadcasting at 1080p via [ATSC 3.0](/wiki/ATSC_3.0 "ATSC 3.0") multiplex stations. One notable example is [WRAL-TV](/wiki/WRAL-TV "WRAL-TV") in [Raleigh, North Carolina](/wiki/Raleigh,_North_Carolina "Raleigh, North Carolina") (a station that re-joined NBC in February 2016), which is currently also broadcasting at 1080p via [WNGT-CD](/wiki/WNGT-CD "WNGT-CD"), which is also serving as an ATSC 3.0 multiplex for the Raleigh area. While the equipment would allow the transmission of 2160p UHD, this was previously done through a secondary experimental station (WRAL-EX) where it transmitted limited NBC programming in UHD. The experimental station went off-air in 2018 as part of the FCC's repacking process.

*[Meet the Press](/wiki/Meet_the_Press "Meet the Press")* was the first regular series on a major television network to produce a high-definition broadcast on February 2, 1997, which aired in the format over [WHD-TV](/wiki/WHD-TV "WHD-TV") in Washington, D.C., an experimental television station owned by a consortium of industry groups and stations which launched to allow testing of HD broadcasts and operated until 2002 (the program itself continued to be transmitted in [480i](/wiki/480i "480i") [standard definition](/wiki/Standard-definition_television "Standard-definition television") over the NBC network until May 2, 2010, when it became the last NBC News program to convert to HD).[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) NBC officially began its conversion to [high definition](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television") with the launch of its simulcast feed, NBC HD, on April 26, 1999, when
*[The Tonight Show](/wiki/The_Tonight_Show "The Tonight Show")* became the first HD program to air on the NBC network as well as the first regularly scheduled American network program to be produced and transmitted in high definition. NBC gradually converted much of its existing programming from [standard-definition](/wiki/Standard-definition_television "Standard-definition television") to high definition beginning with the [2002–03 season](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_United_States_network_television_schedule "2002–03 United States network television schedule"), with select shows among that season's slate of freshmen scripted series being broadcast in HD from their debuts.[[38]](#cite_note-HDTV-38)

NBC completed its conversion to high definition in September 2012, with the launch of NBC Kids, a new Saturday morning children's block programmed by new partial sister network [PBS Kids Sprout](/wiki/Sprout_(TV_network) "Sprout (TV network)"), which also became the second Saturday morning children's block with an entirely HD schedule (after the ABC-syndicated *[Litton's Weekend Adventure](/wiki/Litton%27s_Weekend_Adventure "Litton's Weekend Adventure")*). All the network's programming has been presented in full HD since then (except for certain [holiday](/wiki/Christmas_holiday_season "Christmas holiday season") specials produced prior to 2005 – such as its annual broadcast of *It's a Wonderful Life* – which continues to be presented in 4:3 SD, although some have been remastered for HD broadcast).

The network's high-definition programming is broadcast in [5.1 surround sound](/wiki/5.1_surround_sound "5.1 surround sound").

### NBCi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: NBCi")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/5/5e/NBCi.jpeg)](/wiki/File:NBCi.jpeg)

NBCi header used from 1999 to 2007

In 1999, NBC launched NBCi (briefly changing its web address to "www.nbci.com"), a heavily advertised online venture serving as an attempt to launch a [web portal](/wiki/Web_portal "Web portal"). This move saw NBC partner with [Xoom.com](/wiki/Xoom_(web_hosting) "Xoom (web hosting)") (not to be confused with the current [money transfer service](/wiki/Xoom_Corporation "Xoom Corporation")), e-mail.com, [AllBusiness.com](/wiki/AllBusiness.com "AllBusiness.com"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) and Snap.com (eventually acquiring all four companies outright; not to be confused with the [current-day parent](/wiki/Snap_Inc. "Snap Inc.") of [Snapchat](/wiki/Snapchat "Snapchat")) to launch a multi-faceted internet portal with e-mail, web hosting, community, chat, personalization capabilities, and news content. Subsequently, in April 2000, NBC purchased GlobalBrain, a company specializing in [search engines](/wiki/Search_engine "Search engine") that learned from searches initiated by its users, for $32 million.

The experiment lasted roughly one season; after its failure, NBCi's operations were folded back into NBC.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The NBC Television portion of the website reverted to NBC.com. However, the NBCi website continued in operation as a portal for NBC-branded content (NBCi.com would be redirected to NBCi.msnbc.com), using a co-branded version of [InfoSpace](/wiki/InfoSpace "InfoSpace") to deliver minimal portal content. In mid-2007, NBCi.com began to mirror the main NBC.com website;[[41]](#cite_note-41) NBCi.com was eventually redirected to the NBC.com domain in 2010. Only one legacy of this direction remains in the website of then-O&O [WCMH-TV](/wiki/WCMH-TV "WCMH-TV") in [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio") (now owned by [Nexstar](/wiki/Nexstar_Media_Group "Nexstar Media Group")), which continues to use the URL "nbc4i.com".

## Logo history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Logo history")]

Main article: [NBC logo](/wiki/NBC_logo "NBC logo")

NBC has used a number of logos throughout its history; early logos used by the television and radio networks were similar to the logo of its then-parent company, RCA. Logos used later in NBC's existence incorporated stylized [peacock](/wiki/Peafowl "Peafowl") designs, including the current version that has been in use since 1986.

NBC's current logo features the [colors](/wiki/Colors "Colors"), [Yellow](/wiki/Yellow "Yellow"), [Orange](/wiki/Orange_(color) "Orange (color)"), [Red](/wiki/Red "Red"), [Purple](/wiki/Purple "Purple"), [Cyan](/wiki/Cyan "Cyan"), and [Green](/wiki/Green "Green"), in their own individual positions respectfully. It forms as six-separate feathers for the tail with a peacock's head being white, plus the words, "NBC", are written in a present [sans-serif](/wiki/Sans-serif "Sans-serif") font.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## International broadcasts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: International broadcasts")]

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Canada")]

NBC network programs can be received throughout most of Canada on cable, satellite and [IPTV](/wiki/IPTV "IPTV") providers through certain U.S.-based affiliates of the network (such as [WBTS-CD](/wiki/WBTS-CD "WBTS-CD") in Boston, [KING-TV](/wiki/KING-TV "KING-TV") in Seattle, [KBJR-TV](/wiki/KBJR-TV "KBJR-TV") in [Duluth, Minnesota](/wiki/Duluth,_Minnesota "Duluth, Minnesota"), [WGRZ](/wiki/WGRZ "WGRZ") in [Buffalo, New York](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") and [WHEC-TV](/wiki/WHEC-TV "WHEC-TV") in [Rochester, New York](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York")). Some programs carried on these stations are subject to [simultaneous substitutions](/wiki/Simultaneous_substitution "Simultaneous substitution"), a practice imposed by the [Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission](/wiki/Canadian_Radio-television_and_Telecommunications_Commission "Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission") in which a pay television provider supplants an American station's signal with a feed from a Canadian station/network airing a particular program in the same time slot to protect domestic advertising revenue. Some of these affiliates are also receivable over the air in southern areas of the country located near the [Canada–United States border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border") (signal coverage was somewhat reduced after the [digital television transition in 2009](/wiki/Digital_television_transition_in_the_United_States "Digital television transition in the United States") due to the lower radiated power required to transmit digital signals).

### Europe and the Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Europe and the Middle East")]

NBC no longer exists outside the Americas as a channel in its own right. However, NBC News and MSNBC programs are broadcast for a few hours a day on OSN News, formerly known as [Orbit News](/wiki/Orbit_News "Orbit News") in Africa and the Middle East. Sister network [CNBC Europe](/wiki/CNBC_Europe "CNBC Europe") also broadcasts occasional breaking news coverage from MSNBC as well as *The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon*. [CNBC Europe](/wiki/CNBC_Europe "CNBC Europe") also broadcast daily airings of *NBC Nightly News* at 00:30 CET Monday to Fridays.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

#### NBC Super Channel becomes NBC Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: NBC Super Channel becomes NBC Europe")]

In 1993, then-NBC parent General Electric acquired Super Channel, relaunching the Pan-European cable network as NBC Super Channel.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In 1996, the channel was renamed [NBC Europe](/wiki/NBC_Europe "NBC Europe"), but was, from then on, almost always referred to on-air as simply "NBC".

Most of NBC Europe's prime time programming was produced in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") due to rights restrictions associated with U.S. prime time shows; the channel's weekday late-night schedule after 11:00 p.m. [Central European Time](/wiki/Central_European_Time "Central European Time"), however, featured *The Tonight Show*, *[Late Night with Conan O'Brien](/wiki/Late_Night_with_Conan_O%27Brien "Late Night with Conan O'Brien")* and *[Later](/wiki/Later_(talk_show) "Later (talk show)")*, which the channel's slogan "Where the Stars Come Out at Night" was based around. Many NBC News programs were broadcast on NBC Europe, including *Dateline NBC*, *Meet the Press* and *NBC Nightly News*, the latter of which was broadcast simultaneously with the initial U.S. telecast. *Today* was also initially aired live in the afternoons, but was later broadcast instead the following morning on a more than half-day delay.

In 1999, NBC Europe ceased broadcasting in most of Europe outside of Germany; the network was concurrently relaunched as a German-language technology channel aimed at a younger demographic, with the new series *NBC GIGA* as its flagship program. In 2005, the channel was relaunched again as the [free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") movie channel [Das Vierte](/wiki/Das_Vierte "Das Vierte") which eventually shut down end of 2013 (acquired by Disney, which replaced it with a German version of Disney Channel). [GIGA Television](/wiki/GIGA_Television "GIGA Television") was subsequently spun off as a separate digital channel, available on satellite and cable providers in Germany, Austria and Switzerland, which shut down as a TV station in the end of 2009.

### Latin America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Latin America")]

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Mexico")]

NBC programming is available in Mexico through free-to-air affiliates in markets located within proximity to the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border") (such as [KYMA-DT](/wiki/KYMA-DT "KYMA-DT")/[Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"); [KGNS-TV](/wiki/KGNS-TV "KGNS-TV")/[Laredo, Texas](/wiki/Laredo,_Texas "Laredo, Texas"); [KTSM](/wiki/KTSM-TV "KTSM-TV")/[El Paso, Texas](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas"); [KVEO](/wiki/KVEO "KVEO")/[Brownsville, Texas](/wiki/Brownsville,_Texas "Brownsville, Texas"); and [KNSD](/wiki/KNSD "KNSD")/[San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego")), whose signals are readily receivable over-the-air in border areas of northern Mexico. Some U.S.-based border affiliates are also available on subscription television providers throughout the country, including in the [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") area.

#### Colombia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Colombia")]

In Colombia, many subscription providers carry either select U.S.-based NBC and [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") affiliated stations or the main network feed from [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") or [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo"). Some stations distributing NBC and Telemundo network programming in Colombia include [WTVJ](/wiki/WTVJ "WTVJ") and [WSCV](/wiki/WSCV "WSCV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), and [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") and [WNJU](/wiki/WNJU "WNJU") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").

In early 2017, NBC affiliates stopped being distributed in Colombia. This decision coincided with other U.S. affiliated stations from [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") and [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") also being pulled off from the air in the country. This was due to concerns expressed by the broadcasters on broadcasting rights outside their original local coverage area.

#### Venezuela

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Venezuela")]

In Venezuela, many subscription providers carry either select U.S.-based NBC and [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") affiliated stations or the main network feed from [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") and [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo"). Some stations distributing NBC and Telemundo network programming in Venezuela include [WTVJ](/wiki/WTVJ "WTVJ") and [WSCV](/wiki/WSCV "WSCV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), and [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") and [WNJU](/wiki/WNJU "WNJU") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").

Some stations distributing NBC and Telemundo network programming in Nicaragua and the rest of Central America include [WTVJ](/wiki/WTVJ "WTVJ") and [WSCV](/wiki/WSCV "WSCV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), and [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") and [WNJU](/wiki/WNJU "WNJU") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").

In late 2017, NBC affiliates stopped being distributed in Nicaragua and the rest of Central America. This decision coincided with other U.S. affiliated stations from [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") and [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") also being pulled off from the air in the region. This was due to concerns expressed by the broadcasters on broadcasting rights outside their original local coverage area.

#### Ecuador

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Ecuador")]

In Ecuador, many subscription providers carry either select U.S.-based NBC and [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") affiliated stations or the main network feed from [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") or [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo"). Some stations distributing NBC and Telemundo network programming in Ecuador include [WTVJ](/wiki/WTVJ "WTVJ") and [WSCV](/wiki/WSCV "WSCV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), and [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") and [WNJU](/wiki/WNJU "WNJU") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").

#### Peru

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Peru")]

In Peru, many subscription providers carry either select U.S.-based NBC and [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") affiliated stations or the main network feed from [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") or [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo"). Some stations distributing NBC and Telemundo network programming in Peru include [WTVJ](/wiki/WTVJ "WTVJ") and [WSCV](/wiki/WSCV "WSCV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), and [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") and [WNJU](/wiki/WNJU "WNJU") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").

#### Canal de Noticias

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Canal de Noticias")]

In 1993, NBC launched a 24-hour Spanish-language news channel serving Latin America (the second news channel serving that region overall, after [Noticias ECO](/wiki/Noticias_ECO "Noticias ECO"), and the first to broadcast 24 hours a day), Canal de Noticias NBC, which based its news schedule around the "wheel" format conceived at CNN.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The channel, which was headquartered in the offices of the NBC News Channel affiliate news service in [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"), employed over 50 journalists to produce, write, anchor and provide technical services. Canal de Noticias NBC shut down in 1999 due to the channel's inability to generate sustainable advertising revenue.

### Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Caribbean")]

In the Caribbean, many subscription providers carry either select U.S.-based NBC-affiliated stations or the main network feed from NBC and [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") O&Os [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") and [WNJU](/wiki/WNJU "WNJU") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") or [WTVJ](/wiki/WTVJ "WTVJ") and [WSCV](/wiki/WSCV "WSCV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"). In addition, the network's programming has been available in the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/U.S._Virgin_Islands "U.S. Virgin Islands") since 2004 on [WVGN-LD](/wiki/WVGN-LD "WVGN-LD") in [Charlotte Amalie](/wiki/Charlotte_Amalie,_U.S._Virgin_Islands "Charlotte Amalie, U.S. Virgin Islands") (owned by LKK Group), while Telemundo owned-and-operated station [WKAQ-TV](/wiki/WKAQ-TV "WKAQ-TV") in [San Juan, Puerto Rico](/wiki/San_Juan,_Puerto_Rico "San Juan, Puerto Rico") carries the [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") feed on a digital subchannel.

#### Bahamas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Bahamas")]

In the [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas"), NBC programming is available via U.S.-based affiliate stations on domestic cable providers.

#### Netherlands Antilles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Netherlands Antilles")]

Until its bankruptcy and cessation of operations in 2016,[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) NBC maintained an affiliation in [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba") with [Oranjestad](/wiki/Oranjestad,_Aruba "Oranjestad, Aruba") station [PJA-TV](/wiki/PJA-TV "PJA-TV") (which branded on-air as "15 ATV"). In [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), Telemundo O&O [WKAQ-TV](/wiki/WKAQ-TV "WKAQ-TV") carries "NBC Puerto Rico" over their third subchannel, which is effectively a simulcast of WNBC with some local advertising and station identification.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Bermuda")]

Until it ended operations in 2014, NBC's entire program lineup was carried by [VSB-TV](/wiki/VSB-TV "VSB-TV"), using the [Eastern Time Zone](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") feed, though an hour ahead due to its location in the [Atlantic Time Zone](/wiki/Atlantic_Time_Zone "Atlantic Time Zone"). ### Pacific

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Pacific")]

#### Guam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Guam")]

In [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), the entire NBC programming lineup is carried by [Hagåtña](/wiki/Hag%C3%A5t%C3%B1a,_Guam "Hagåtña, Guam") affiliate [KUAM-TV](/wiki/KUAM-TV "KUAM-TV") (which has been an NBC affiliate since 1956) via the network's East Coast satellite feed. Entertainment and news programming is broadcast day and date on a one-day tape delay as Guam is on the west side of the [International Date Line](/wiki/International_Date_Line "International Date Line") (for example, the network's Thursday prime time lineup airs Friday evenings on KUAM, and is advertised by the station as airing on the latter night in on-air promotions). Live programming, including breaking news and sporting events, airs as scheduled; because of the time difference with the six U.S. time zones, live sports coverage often airs on the station early in the morning. KUAM's programming is relayed to the [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands") via [satellite station](/wiki/Broadcast_relay_station#satellite_stations "Broadcast relay station") [WSZE](/wiki/WSZE "WSZE") in [Saipan](/wiki/Saipan "Saipan").

#### American Samoa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: American Samoa")]

In [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa"), NBC was affiliated with [KKHJ-LP](/wiki/KBAD-LD "KBAD-LD") in [Pago Pago](/wiki/Pago_Pago "Pago Pago")[[50]](#cite_note-50) from 2005 to 2012. Cable television providers on the islands carry the network's programming via Seattle affiliate KING-TV.

#### Federated States of Micronesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Federated States of Micronesia")]

In the [Federated States of Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia"), NBC programming is available on domestic cable providers via [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu") affiliate [KHNL](/wiki/KHNL "KHNL").

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### NBC Asia and CNBC Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: NBC Asia and CNBC Asia")]

NBC Asia launched in 1994, distributed to [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). Like NBC Europe, NBC Asia featured most of NBC's news programs as well as *The Tonight Show*, *Late Night* and *Saturday Night Live*. Like its European counterpart, it was not allowed to broadcast American-produced prime time shows due to existing broadcast agreements with other domestic broadcasters. NBC Asia produced a regional evening news program that aired each weeknight, and occasionally simulcast some programs from [CNBC Asia](/wiki/CNBC_Asia "CNBC Asia") and MSNBC. NBC also operated NBC Super Sports, a 24-hour channel devoted to televising sporting events.

In July 1998, NBC Asia was replaced by a regional version of the [National Geographic Channel](/wiki/National_Geographic_Channel_(Asia) "National Geographic Channel (Asia)").

#### Regional partners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Regional partners")]

Through regional partners, NBC-produced programs are seen in some countries on the continent. In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Jack TV](/wiki/Jack_TV "Jack TV") (owned by Solar Entertainment) airs *Will & Grace* and *Saturday Night Live*, while [TalkTV](/wiki/Talktv_(Philippines) "Talktv (Philippines)") airs *The Tonight Show* and NBC News programs including the weekday and [weekend](/wiki/Weekend_Today "Weekend Today") editions of *Today*, *Early Today*, *Dateline NBC* and *NBC Nightly News*. [Solar TV](/wiki/Solar_TV "Solar TV") formerly broadcast *The Jay Leno Show* from 2009 to 2010. In Hong Kong, the English language free-to-air channel TVB Pearl (operated by [TVB](/wiki/TVB "TVB")) airs live broadcasts of *NBC Nightly News*, as well as other select NBC programs.

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Australia")]

In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the [Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") has maintained close ties with NBC and has used a majority of the U.S. network's image campaigns and slogans since the 1970s (conversely, in 2009, NBC and Seven both used the [Guy Sebastian](/wiki/Guy_Sebastian "Guy Sebastian") single "[Like it Like That](/wiki/Like_It_like_That_(Guy_Sebastian_song) "Like It like That (Guy Sebastian song)")" in image promos for their respective summer schedules). The network's *[Seven News](/wiki/Seven_News "Seven News")* division has used John Williams-composed "The Mission" (the proprietary theme music for NBC News' flagship programs since 1985) as the theme music for its local and national news programs since the mid-1980s, though re-composed domestically to meet their own branding image. Local newscasts were also titled *Seven Nightly News* from the mid-1980s until c. 2000. NBC News and Seven News often share news resources, with the former division using Seven's reporters for breaking news coverage and select taped story packages relating to Australian stories and the latter sometimes incorporating NBC News reports into its national bulletins.

Seven also rebroadcasts some of NBC's news and current affairs programming during the early morning hours (usually from 3:00 to 5:00 a.m. local time), including the weekday and weekend editions of *Today* (which it brands as *NBC Today* to differentiate it from the unrelated [morning program of the same title](/wiki/Today_(1982_TV_program) "Today (1982 TV program)") on the [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network")), *Dateline NBC* and *Meet the Press*.

## Criticism and controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=NBC&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Criticism and controversies")]

See also: [Today (American TV program) § Controversies](/wiki/Today_(American_TV_program)#Controversies "Today (American TV program)"), and [MSNBC criticisms and controversies](/wiki/MSNBC_criticisms_and_controversies "MSNBC criticisms and controversies")

During the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") NBC received criticism of its reporting of the conflict.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In March and April 2019, the *[Huffington Post](/wiki/Huffington_Post "Huffington Post")* and *[Wired](/wiki/Wired_(magazine) "Wired (magazine)")* reported that NBC had paid a firm to [improve its reputation](/wiki/Reputation_management "Reputation management") by [lobbying for changes](/wiki/Conflict-of-interest_editing_on_Wikipedia "Conflict-of-interest editing on Wikipedia") to the [Wikipedia](/wiki/Wikipedia "Wikipedia") articles on NBC, [Nextdoor](/wiki/Nextdoor "Nextdoor") and several others.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

The NBC television network has been accused[[54]](#cite_note-54) of tolerating a culture of [sexism](/wiki/Sexism "Sexism") and [sexual harassment](/wiki/Sexual_harassment "Sexual harassment") among its employees (especially within upper management and among senior anchors such as [Matt Lauer](/wiki/Matt_Lauer "Matt Lauer")) and also of covering up indiscretions committed by prominent figures in the company through intimidation campaigns against victims that include widespread use of [non-disclosure agreements](/wiki/Non-disclosure_agreements "Non-disclosure agreements"). This may have exposed the company to pressure from [Harvey Weinstein](/wiki/Harvey_Weinstein "Harvey Weinstein") to delay or terminate reporting on [Weinstein's criminal abuse of many women](/wiki/Harvey_Weinstein_sexual_abuse_cases "Harvey Weinstein sexual abuse cases").[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Presidents of NBC Entertainment
